***The Wall Street Journal*** (***WSJ***), commonly known as **the *Journal***, is an American newspaper based in [Midtown Manhattan](/wiki/Midtown_Manhattan "Midtown Manhattan"), New York City. It provides extensive coverage of news, especially business and finance, and operates on a subscription model that requires readers to pay for access to most articles and other content. The *Journal* is published six days a week by [Dow Jones & Company](/wiki/Dow_Jones_%26_Company "Dow Jones & Company"), a division of [News Corp](/wiki/News_Corp "News Corp").

As of 2025, *The* *Wall Street Journal* is the [largest newspaper in the United States](/wiki/List_of_newspapers_in_the_United_States "List of newspapers in the United States") by [print circulation](/wiki/Print_circulation "Print circulation"), with 412,000 print subscribers. It has 4.13 million digital subscribers, the second-most in the nation after *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")*.[[1]](#cite_note-circ-2025-1) The newspaper is one of the United States' [newspapers of record](/wiki/Newspaper_of_record "Newspaper of record").[[2]](#cite_note-caulfield1-2)[[3]](#cite_note-FrostWeingarten20172-3) The first issue of the newspaper was published on July 8, 1889.[[4]](#cite_note-4) The [editorial page of the *Journal*](/wiki/Editorial_board_at_The_Wall_Street_Journal "Editorial board at The Wall Street Journal") is typically [center-right](/wiki/Center-right "Center-right") in its positions, while its news reporting is more centrist.[[5]](#cite_note-Benkler-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-Vernon_2017-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) The newspaper has won 39 [Pulitzer Prizes](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize "Pulitzer Prize").[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wall_Street_Journal&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Founding and 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wall_Street_Journal&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Founding and 19th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/The_Wall_Street_Journal_first_issue.jpg/250px-The_Wall_Street_Journal_first_issue.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Wall_Street_Journal_first_issue.jpg)

Front page of the first issue of *The Wall Street Journal* on July 8, 1889

A predecessor to *The Wall Street Journal* was the Kiernan News Agency founded by [John J. Kiernan](/wiki/John_J._Kiernan "John J. Kiernan") in 1869.[[13]](#cite_note-Wells-13): 321, 324 In 1880, Kiernan hired [Charles H. Dow](/wiki/Charles_H._Dow "Charles H. Dow") and [Edward D. Jones](/wiki/Edward_Jones_(statistician) "Edward Jones (statistician)") as reporters. On a recommendation of [Collis Potter Huntington](/wiki/Collis_Potter_Huntington "Collis Potter Huntington"), Dow and Jones co-founded their own news service, [Dow Jones and Company](/wiki/Dow_Jones_and_Company "Dow Jones and Company"), with fellow Kiernan reporter [Charles Bergstresser](/wiki/Charles_Bergstresser "Charles Bergstresser"). Dow Jones was headquartered in the basement of 15 Wall Street, the same building as Kiernan's company next to the [New York Stock Exchange Building](/wiki/New_York_Stock_Exchange_Building "New York Stock Exchange Building").[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScharff1986[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage2mode2upq22johnjkiernan22_2-3]-14)

The first products of [Dow Jones & Company](/wiki/Dow_Jones_%26_Company "Dow Jones & Company"), the publisher of the *Journal*, were brief news bulletins, nicknamed *flimsies*, hand-delivered throughout the day to traders at the [stock exchange](/wiki/Stock_exchange "Stock exchange").[[15]](#cite_note-late_1800s-15) In 1883, they were aggregated in a printed daily summary called the *Customers' Afternoon Letter*, sold for $1.50 per month compared to the $15 a month Dow Jones bulletin service.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScharff1986[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage4mode2upq22afternoonletter22_4]-16) Dow Jones opened its own printing press at [71 Broadway](/wiki/Empire_Building_(Manhattan) "Empire Building (Manhattan)") in 1885.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScharff1986[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage4mode2upq22afternoonletter22_4]-16)

Beginning July 8, 1889, the *Afternoon Letter* was renamed *The Wall Street Journal*. The debut issue of the *Journal* was four pages long, with dimensions of 20 3/4 × 15 1/2 inches and cost of $0.02 per copy. In its early days, the *Journal* had "a tedious, blow-by-blow account of the day's business without benefit of editing," wrote Edward E. Scharff in 1986.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScharff1986[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage4mode2upq22afternoonletter22_4]-16)

For nearly 40 years, the front page had a four-column format, with the middle two devoted to news briefs and the farther two filled with advertisements for brokerage services. The *Journal* focused on stories from news wires and listings of stocks and bonds, while occasionally covering sports or politics.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScharff1986[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage4mode2upq22afternoonletter22_4]-16) One front-page story on the debut edition of *The Wall Street Journal* was a raw wire report about the boxing match between [John L. Sullivan](/wiki/John_L._Sullivan "John L. Sullivan") and [Jake Kilrain](/wiki/Jake_Kilrain "Jake Kilrain"), with varying accounts of the fight citing *[The Boston Globe](/wiki/The_Boston_Globe "The Boston Globe")*, the *[Baltimore American](/wiki/Baltimore_American "Baltimore American")*, and anonymous sources.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScharff1986[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage4mode2upq22sullivanandkilrain22_5]-17) Seldom did *The Wall Street Journal* publish analysis or opinion articles in its early decades.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScharff1986[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage6mode2upq22analysiswasrare22_6]-18) In addition to a private wire to [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston"), the *Journal* had reporters communicate via telegraph from Washington, Philadelphia, Chicago, Pittsburgh, [Albany](/wiki/Albany,_New_York "Albany, New York"), and London.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScharff1986[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage8mode2upqalbany_8]-19)

In 1896, the *Journal* began publishing two separate Dow Jones stock indicies, the [Dow Jones Industrial Average](/wiki/Dow_Jones_Industrial_Average "Dow Jones Industrial Average") and [Dow Jones Railroad Average](/wiki/Dow_Jones_Transportation_Average "Dow Jones Transportation Average").[[15]](#cite_note-late_1800s-15)[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScharff1986[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage6mode2upq22twoseparateindexes22_7]-[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage8mode2upq22oneforindustrials22_8]-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21) The first morning edition of the *Journal* was published on November 14, 1898. By the late 1890s, daily circulation reached 7,000.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScharff1986[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage6mode2upq22analysiswasrare22_6]-18)

Charles Dow wrote the first "Review and Outlook" column on April 21, 1899, a front-page editorial column explaining stock prices in terms of human nature; Dow's thinking would later be known as the [Dow theory](/wiki/Dow_theory "Dow theory"). Scharff regarded Dow's essays from 1899 to 1902 as "stock market classics".[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScharff1986[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage6mode2upq22Humannature22_6-_7]-22)

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wall_Street_Journal&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 20th century")]

In the months before his death in 1902, Dow arranged to sell Dow Jones and the *Journal* to [Clarence W. Barron](/wiki/Clarence_W._Barron "Clarence W. Barron"), the [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston") correspondent for the *Journal* since 1889, for $130,000 (equivalent to $4,837,500 in 2025).[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScharff1986[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage8mode2upq22in1902at22_9]-[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage10mode2upq22clarencewalkerbarron22_10]-23) Because Barron had financial difficulties, his wife Jessie Waldron Barron made the $2,500 down payment to buy Dow Jones in 1902; Clarence would first own a Dow Jones share about ten years later.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScharff1986[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage14mode2upq22jessiewaldron22_14]-24)

Under Barron's ownership, [Thomas F. Woodlock](/wiki/Thomas_F._Woodlock "Thomas F. Woodlock") was editor of the *Journal* from 1902 to 1905.[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26) By the end of Woodlock's tenure, daily circulation for the *Journal* rose from 7,000 to 11,000.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScharff1986[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage14mode2upq22anestimated112C00022_15]-27) [William Peter Hamilton](/wiki/William_Peter_Hamilton "William Peter Hamilton") became lead editorial writer in 1908, a time when the *Journal* began reflecting the views of Barron. Hamilton wrote what Scharff considered "daily sermons in support of free-market capitalism".[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScharff1986[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage16mode2upq22dailysermons22_16]-28)

Barron and his predecessors were credited with creating an atmosphere of fearless, independent financial reporting—a novelty in the early days of [business journalism](/wiki/Business_journalism "Business journalism"). In 1921, *[Barron's](/wiki/Barron%27s_(newspaper) "Barron's (newspaper)")*, the United States's premier financial weekly, was founded.[[29]](#cite_note-Crossen-29) Scharff described the newspaper in the Barron era as "Wall Street's public defender" against regulatory efforts by the U.S. Congress.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScharff1986[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage26mode2upq22Wallstreet27spublicdefender22_27]-30) Circulation continued to rise, reaching 18,750 to 1920 and 52,000 briefly in 1928.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScharff1986[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage14mode2upq22anestimated112C00022_15]-27) Barron died in 1928, a year before [Black Tuesday](/wiki/Wall_Street_Crash_of_1929 "Wall Street Crash of 1929"), the stock market crash that greatly affected the [Great Depression in the United States](/wiki/Great_Depression_in_the_United_States "Great Depression in the United States"). Barron's descendants, the [Bancroft family](/wiki/Bancroft_family "Bancroft family"), would continue to control the company until 2007.[[29]](#cite_note-Crossen-29)

The [Wall Street Crash of 1929](/wiki/Wall_Street_Crash_of_1929 "Wall Street Crash of 1929") would be yet another challenge to the *Journal* on top of Barron's death. William Peter Hamilton died of pneumonia on December 9, 1929, aged 63.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScharff1986[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage34mode2upq22pacificcoastedition22_34]-31) Circulation of the *Journal*, having surpassed 50,000 in 1928, dropped below 28,000 in the 1930s, and the newspaper downsized from 28 to 16 pages in the 1930s as well.[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScharff1986[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage36mode2upq22numberofpageswouldalsofall22_36]-32) Dow Jones president [Hugh Bancroft](/wiki/Hugh_Bancroft_(attorney) "Hugh Bancroft (attorney)") retired in 1932; following his death in 1933, his widow Jane Waldron Bancroft appointed [Kenneth Craven "Casey" Hogate](/wiki/Kenneth_C._Hogate "Kenneth C. Hogate") as new company president.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScharff1986[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage42mode2upq22metwithcaseyhogate22_43]-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34) Hogate envisioned expanding the scope of the *Journal* to a "more general business paper" beyond stock and bond numbers.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScharff1986[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage50mode2upq22moregeneralbusinesspaper22_50]-35)

Expanding westward, *The Wall Street Journal* debuted a West Coast edition on October 21, 1929, *The Wall Street Journal Pacific Coast Edition*. The *Pacific Coast Edition* focused on California businesses and replicated some items from the regular *Wall Street Journal*; however, its circulation never exceeded 3,000, and the Great Depression led numerous subscription cancellations.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScharff1986[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage34mode2upq22pacificcoastedition22_34],_[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage50mode2upq22pacificcoastedition22_50]-36)

By 1931, [Bernard Kilgore](/wiki/Bernard_Kilgore "Bernard Kilgore") became news editor for *The Wall Street Journal*, having joined the *Journal* copy desk in 1929. He began writing a column for the *Pacific Coast Edition* called "Dear George", a feature explaining obscure financial topics in simpler, plain rhetoric. "Dear George" sharply contrasted with other *Wall Street Journal* articles that relied on jargon that was incomprehensible even to its own reporters. The Eastern edition of the *Journal* began carrying "Dear George", and beginning in 1932, Kilgore wrote "Dear George" three times a week from New York for the *Journal*.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScharff1986[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage50mode2upq22bombaystraddle22_51]-[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage52mode2upq22Deargeorge22_52]-37) Then in 1934, Kilgore began writing a daily news digest "What's News" for the *Journal* front page.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScharff1986[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage52mode2upq22themajorstoriesoftheday22_53]-38) Kilgore's writings attracted the attention of the White House; President [Franklin D. Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Franklin D. Roosevelt") publicly recommended Kilgore's work about pension payments for World War I veterans and a Supreme Court decision on the [National Industrial Recovery Act of 1933](/wiki/National_Industrial_Recovery_Act_of_1933 "National Industrial Recovery Act of 1933").[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScharff1986[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage52mode2upq22readkilgore22_53]-39) However, the *Journal* continued to struggle financially, with circulation stagnant at 32,000 in 1940. Most editions were only 12 to 14 pages long, and Dow Jones made only $69,000 of profits on $2 million of revenue, mostly due to its news ticker. Scharff observed a lack of coverage about a possible U.S. role in [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScharff1986[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage60mode2upq22attentionwasrivetedoneurope22_60]-40)

In the 1940s, Dow Jones took steps to restructure the *Journal*. Kilgore was named managing editor of the *Journal* in 1941 and Dow Jones CEO in 1945.[[29]](#cite_note-Crossen-29) Scharff described Kilgore's approach to journalism: "A *Journal* story had to satisfy its sophisticated readership, but it also had to be clear enough not to discourage neophytes."[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScharff1986[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage62mode2upq22satisfyitssophisticated22_63]-[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage64mode2upq22neverdisplacethehometown22_64]-41) Additionally, Kilgore aimed to have the newspaper appeal to a national audience, by making the East Coast and West Coast editions of the *Journal* more homogeneous.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScharff1986[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage62mode2upq22satisfyitssophisticated22_63]-[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage64mode2upq22neverdisplacethehometown22_64]-41) Until printing presses and reporting bureaus could be opened on location, photographs could not be included in the *Journal*.[[29]](#cite_note-Crossen-29)

In [1947](/wiki/1947_Pulitzer_Prize "1947 Pulitzer Prize"), the paper won its first [Pulitzer Prize](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize "Pulitzer Prize") for William Henry Grimes's [editorials](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize_for_Editorial_Writing "Pulitzer Prize for Editorial Writing").[[29]](#cite_note-Crossen-29) Also in May 1947, the *Journal* launched a Southwest edition based in [Dallas](/wiki/Dallas "Dallas").[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScharff1986[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage102mode2upqdallas_103]-[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage102mode2upqdallas_105]-42) The newspaper also added a new slogan in 1949: "Everywhere, Men Who Get Ahead in Business Read *The Wall Street Journal*."[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScharff1986[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage102mode2upqdallas_103]-[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage102mode2upqdallas_105]-42) Circulation grew by nearly four-fold, from 32,000 in 1940 to 140,000 in 1949; however, Kilgore's editorial reforms of the *Journal* had not yet entered popular understanding.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScharff1986[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage60mode2upq22attentionwasrivetedoneurope22_60],_[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage104mode2upq22over1402C00022_104]-43)

The first major journalism award for the *Journal* was a [Sigma Delta Chi](/wiki/Society_of_Professional_Journalists "Society of Professional Journalists") public service award, for stories in late 1952 exposing links between Empire Mail Order and organized crime. These stories followed news that [Howard Hughes](/wiki/Howard_Hughes "Howard Hughes") sold [RKO Pictures](/wiki/RKO_Pictures "RKO Pictures") to Empire Mail Order.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScharff1986[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage120mode2upq22howardhughes22_121]-[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage122mode2upqrko_122]-44) Circulation of the *Journal* continued increasing throughout the 1950s, to 205,000 in 1951, 400,000 in 1955, and over 500,000 by 1957.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScharff1986[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage154mode2upq22halfmillionmark22_154]-45)

[Warren H. Phillips](/wiki/Warren_H._Phillips "Warren H. Phillips") became managing editor of *The Wall Street Journal* in 1957 after being promoted from Chicago bureau manager. Phillips was Jewish, in contrast to the largely midwestern, [WASP](/wiki/WASP "WASP") management of the *Journal* at the time. Phillips, a former socialist, shifted his political views in the 1950s to reflect social liberalism and fiscal conservatism.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScharff1986[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage166mode2upq22warrenphillipsbecame22_167]-[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage168mode2upq22tragicomicspectacle22_169]-46) Under Phillips, the *Journal* provided in-depth coverage of the [civil rights movement](/wiki/Civil_rights_movement "Civil rights movement"), on the grounds that it "was something that the average businessman needed to know about".[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScharff1986[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage166mode2upq22warrenphillipsbecame22_167]-[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage168mode2upq22tragicomicspectacle22_169]-46) For instance, *Journal* coverage of the [Little Rock Central High School integration crisis](/wiki/Little_Rock_Nine "Little Rock Nine") went beyond the largely visual, emotional elements on newspapers and television, in depicting local citizens as supportive of integration in contrast to Governor [Orval Faubus](/wiki/Orval_Faubus "Orval Faubus") and other local politicians.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScharff1986[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage170mode2upq22governorfaubushimself22_170-171]-47)

During the [1962–1963 New York City newspaper strike](/wiki/1962%E2%80%931963_New_York_City_newspaper_strike "1962–1963 New York City newspaper strike"), the *Journal* was the only daily newspaper to continue printing in New York City; however, after the strike, Kilgore ordered the printing presses moved from New York to [Chicopee, Massachusetts](/wiki/Chicopee,_Massachusetts "Chicopee, Massachusetts") effective July 1, 1963. Although Kilgore did not publicly explain his rationale then, Scharff wrote in 1986 that the move resulted from feuds between Dow Jones and the printer's union.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScharff1986[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage118mode2upqchicopee_118-119]-48)

By Kilgore's death in 1967, *Wall Street Journal* circulation exceeded one million.[[29]](#cite_note-Crossen-29) In 1967, Dow Jones Newswires began a major expansion outside of the United States ultimately placing its journalists in every major financial center in [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"), [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia"), [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America"), [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), and [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa"). In 1970, Dow Jones bought the Ottaway newspaper chain, which at the time comprised nine dailies and three Sunday newspapers. Later, the name was changed to [Dow Jones Local Media Group](/wiki/Local_Media_Group "Local Media Group").[[49]](#cite_note-49) The first strike affecting a *Journal* printing plant was a three-day strike at [Silver Spring, Maryland](/wiki/Silver_Spring,_Maryland "Silver Spring, Maryland"), in 1967; that would be followed by a weeklong strike by truck drivers at the [South Brunswick, New Jersey](/wiki/South_Brunswick,_New_Jersey "South Brunswick, New Jersey"), in 1970.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScharff1986[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage118mode2upqchicopee_118-119]-48)

Video of U.S. President [Ronald Reagan](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan "Ronald Reagan") interviewed by *WSJ* in the [Oval Office](/wiki/Oval_Office "Oval Office") in February 1985

The period from 1971 to 1997 brought about a series of launches, acquisitions, and joint ventures, including "[Factiva](/wiki/Factiva "Factiva")", *[The Wall Street Journal Asia](/wiki/The_Wall_Street_Journal_Asia "The Wall Street Journal Asia")*, *[The Wall Street Journal Europe](/wiki/The_Wall_Street_Journal_Europe "The Wall Street Journal Europe")*, the WSJ.com website, Dow Jones Indexes, [MarketWatch](/wiki/MarketWatch "MarketWatch"), and "WSJ Weekend Edition". In 2007, [News Corp.](/wiki/News_Corporation "News Corporation") acquired Dow Jones.[[50]](#cite_note-50) *[WSJ.](/wiki/WSJ. "WSJ.")*, a luxury lifestyle magazine, was launched in 2008.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

A complement to the print newspaper, *The Wall Street Journal Online*, was launched in 1996 and has allowed access only by subscription from the beginning.[[52]](#cite_note-52) A weekly (later daily) crossword edited by [Mike Shenk](/wiki/Mike_Shenk "Mike Shenk") was introduced in 1998.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wall_Street_Journal&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 21st century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/Vladimir_Putin_11_February_2002-1.jpg/250px-Vladimir_Putin_11_February_2002-1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vladimir_Putin_11_February_2002-1.jpg)

[Vladimir Putin](/wiki/Vladimir_Putin "Vladimir Putin") with *Wall Street Journal* correspondent [Karen Elliott House](/wiki/Karen_Elliott_House "Karen Elliott House") in 2002

In 2003, Dow Jones began to integrate reporting of the *Journal*'s print and online subscribers together in [Audit Bureau of Circulations](/wiki/Audit_Bureau_of_Circulations_(North_America) "Audit Bureau of Circulations (North America)") statements.[[54]](#cite_note-54) In 2007, it was commonly believed to be the largest paid-subscription news site on the Web, with 980,000 paid subscribers.[[29]](#cite_note-Crossen-29) Since then, digital subscription has risen to 1.3 million as of September 2018, falling to number two behind *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* with 3 million digital subscriptions.[[55]](#cite_note-55) In May 2008, an annual subscription to the [digital edition](/wiki/Digital_edition "Digital edition") of *The Wall Street Journal* cost $119 for those who do not have subscriptions to the print edition. By June 2013, the monthly cost for a subscription to the online edition was $22.99, or $275.88 annually, excluding introductory offers.[[56]](#cite_note-56) Digital subscription rates increased dramatically to $443.88 per year as its popularity increased over print, with first-time subscribers paying $187.20 per year.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

On November 30, 2004, Oasys Mobile and *The Wall Street Journal* released an app that would allow users to access content from *The Wall Street Journal Online* via their mobile phones.[[58]](#cite_note-58)

In September 2005, the *Journal* launched a weekend edition, delivered to all subscribers, which marked a return to Saturday publication after a lapse of some 50 years. The move was designed in part to attract more consumer advertising.[[29]](#cite_note-Crossen-29)

In 2005, the *Journal* reported a readership profile of about 60 percent top management, an average income of $191,000, an average household net worth of $2.1 million, and an average age of 55.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

In 2007, the *Journal* launched a worldwide expansion of its website to include major foreign-language editions. The paper had also shown an interest in buying the rival *[Financial Times](/wiki/Financial_Times "Financial Times")*.[[60]](#cite_note-60)

### Design changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wall_Street_Journal&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Design changes")]

The nameplate is unique in having a period at the end.[[61]](#cite_note-61)

Front-page advertising in the *Journal* was re-introduced on September 5, 2006. This followed similar introductions in the European and Asian editions in late 2005.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

After presenting nearly identical front-page layouts for half a century – always six columns, with the day's top stories in the first and sixth columns, "What's News" digest in the second and third, the "A-hed" feature story in the fourth (with 'hed' being jargon for [headline](/wiki/Headline "Headline")) and themed weekly reports in the fifth column[[63]](#cite_note-63) – the paper in 2007 decreased its [broadsheet](/wiki/Broadsheet "Broadsheet") width from 15 to 12 inches while keeping the length at 223⁄4 inches, to save [newsprint](/wiki/Newsprint "Newsprint") costs. News design consultant [Mario García](/wiki/Mario_Garc%C3%ADa_(designer) "Mario García (designer)") collaborated on the changes. Dow Jones said it would save $18 million a year in newsprint costs across all *The Wall Street Journal* papers.[[64]](#cite_note-64) This move eliminated one column of print, pushing the "A-hed" out of its traditional location (though the paper now usually includes a quirky feature story on the right side of the front page, sandwiched among the lead stories).

The paper uses ink dot drawings called [hedcuts](/wiki/Hedcut "Hedcut"), introduced in 1979 and originally created by [Kevin Sprouls](/wiki/Kevin_Sprouls "Kevin Sprouls"),[[65]](#cite_note-65) in addition to photographs, a method of illustration considered a consistent visual signature of the paper. the *Journal* still heavily employs the use of [caricatures](/wiki/Caricatures "Caricatures"), including those by illustrator [Ken Fallin](/wiki/Ken_Fallin "Ken Fallin"), such as when [Peggy Noonan](/wiki/Peggy_Noonan "Peggy Noonan") memorialized then-recently deceased newsman [Tim Russert](/wiki/Tim_Russert "Tim Russert").[[66]](#cite_note-66)[[67]](#cite_note-67) The use of color photographs and graphics has become increasingly common in recent years with the addition of more "lifestyle" sections.

The daily was awarded by the [Society for News Design](/wiki/Society_for_News_Design "Society for News Design") World's Best Designed Newspaper award for 1994 and 1997.[[68]](#cite_note-68)

### News Corporation and News Corp

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wall_Street_Journal&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: News Corporation and News Corp")]

On May 2, 2007, [News Corporation](/wiki/News_Corporation "News Corporation") made an unsolicited takeover bid for [Dow Jones](/wiki/Dow_Jones_%26_Company "Dow Jones & Company"), offering $60 per share for stock that had been selling for $36.33 per share.[[69]](#cite_note-69) The [Bancroft family](/wiki/Bancroft_family "Bancroft family"), which controlled more than 60% of the voting stock, at first rejected the offer, but later reconsidered its position.[[70]](#cite_note-70)

Three months later, on August 1, 2007, News Corporation and Dow Jones entered into a definitive merger agreement.[[71]](#cite_note-71) The $5 billion sale added *The Wall Street Journal* to [Rupert Murdoch](/wiki/Rupert_Murdoch "Rupert Murdoch")'s news empire, which already included [Fox News Channel](/wiki/Fox_News_Channel "Fox News Channel"), [Fox Business Network](/wiki/Fox_Business_Network "Fox Business Network"), London's *[The Times](/wiki/The_Times "The Times")*, the *[New York Post](/wiki/New_York_Post "New York Post")*, and the [Fox](/wiki/Fox_Broadcasting_Company "Fox Broadcasting Company") [flagship station](/wiki/Flagship_station "Flagship station") [WNYW](/wiki/WNYW "WNYW") (Channel 5) and [MyNetworkTV](/wiki/MyNetworkTV "MyNetworkTV") flagship [WWOR](/wiki/WWOR-TV "WWOR-TV") (Channel 9).[[72]](#cite_note-72)

On December 13, 2007, shareholders representing more than 60 percent of Dow Jones's voting stock approved the company's acquisition by News Corporation.[[73]](#cite_note-73)

In an editorial page column, publisher [L. Gordon Crovitz](/wiki/L._Gordon_Crovitz "L. Gordon Crovitz") said the Bancrofts and News Corporation had agreed that the *Journal*'s news and opinion sections would preserve their editorial independence from their new corporate parent.[[74]](#cite_note-Crovitz-74)

A special committee was established to oversee the paper's editorial integrity. When the managing editor [Marcus Brauchli](/wiki/Marcus_Brauchli "Marcus Brauchli") resigned on April 22, 2008, the committee said that News Corporation had violated its agreement by not notifying the committee earlier. However, Brauchli said he believed that new owners should appoint their own editor.[[75]](#cite_note-75)

A 2007 *Journal* article quoted charges that Murdoch had made and broken similar promises in the past. One large shareholder commented that Murdoch has long "expressed his personal, political and [business](/wiki/Business "Business") biases through his newspapers and television stations". Former *Times* assistant editor [Fred Emery](/wiki/Fred_Emery_(journalist) "Fred Emery (journalist)") remembers an incident when "Mr. Murdoch called him into his office in March 1982 and said he was considering firing *Times* editor [Harold Evans](/wiki/Harold_Evans "Harold Evans"). Mr. Emery says he reminded Mr. Murdoch of his promise that editors couldn't be fired without the independent directors' approval. 'God, you don't take all that seriously, do you?' Mr. Murdoch answered, according to Mr. Emery." Murdoch eventually forced out Evans.[[76]](#cite_note-76)

In 2011, *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* found evidence that the *Journal* had artificially inflated its European sales numbers, by paying Executive Learning Partnership for purchasing 16% of European sales. These inflated sales numbers then enabled the *Journal* to charge similarly inflated advertising rates, as the advertisers would think that they reached more readers than they actually did. In addition, the *Journal* agreed to run "articles" featuring Executive Learning Partnership, presented as news, but effectively advertising.[[77]](#cite_note-77) The case came to light after a Belgian *Wall Street Journal* employee, [Gert Van Mol](/wiki/Gert_Van_Mol "Gert Van Mol"), informed Dow Jones CEO [Les Hinton](/wiki/Les_Hinton "Les Hinton") about the questionable practice.[[78]](#cite_note-78) As a result, the then *Wall Street Journal Europe* CEO and Publisher Andrew Langhoff was fired after it was found out he personally pressured journalists into covering one of the newspaper's business partners involved in the issue.[[79]](#cite_note-79)[[80]](#cite_note-80) Since September 2011, all the online articles that resulted from the ethical wrongdoing carry a *Wall Street Journal* disclaimer informing the readers about the circumstances in which they were created.

The *Journal*, along with its parent Dow Jones & Company, was among the businesses News Corporation spun off in 2013 as the new [News Corp](/wiki/News_Corp "News Corp").[[81]](#cite_note-81)

In November 2016, in an effort to cut costs, the *Journal*'s editor-in-chief, [Gerard Baker](/wiki/Gerard_Baker "Gerard Baker"), announced layoffs of staff and consolidation of its print sections. The new "Business & Finance" section combined the former "Business & Tech" and "Money & Investing" sections. The new "Life & Arts" section took the place of "Personal Journal" and "Arena". In addition, the *Journal*'s "Greater New York" coverage was reduced and moved to the main section of paper.[[82]](#cite_note-82) The section was shuttered on July 9, 2021.[[83]](#cite_note-nyt-greater-ny-83)

A 2018 survey conducted by [Gallup](/wiki/Gallup_(company) "Gallup (company)") and the [Knight Foundation](/wiki/Knight_Foundation "Knight Foundation") found that *The Wall Street Journal* was considered the third most-accurate and fourth most-unbiased news organization among the general public, tenth among [Democrats](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)"), and second among [Republicans](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)").[[84]](#cite_note-84) In an October 2018 [Simmons Research](/wiki/Simmons_Research "Simmons Research") survey of 38 news organizations, *The Wall Street Journal* was ranked the most trusted news organization by Americans. [Joshua Benton](/wiki/Joshua_Benton "Joshua Benton") of the [Nieman Journalism Lab](/wiki/Nieman_Journalism_Lab "Nieman Journalism Lab") at [Harvard University](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University") wrote that the paper's "combination of respected news pages and conservative editorial pages seem to be a magic formula for generating trust across the ideological spectrum."[[85]](#cite_note-85)

From 2019 through 2022, the *Journal* partnered with [Facebook](/wiki/Facebook "Facebook") to provide content for the social-media site's "News Tab". Facebook paid the *Journal* in excess of $10 million during that period, terminating the relationship as part of a broader shift away from news content.[[86]](#cite_note-86)

On June 13, 2022, the *Journal* launched a [product review website](/wiki/Review_site "Review site") called *Buy Side*.[[87]](#cite_note-axios220622-87) The website remains free and has a distinct team from the *Journal* newsroom.[[87]](#cite_note-axios220622-87)

In February 2024, the *Journal* laid off about 20 employees, primarily economics reporters based in [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.") Moving forward, those beats will be covered by the newspaper's New York-based business team.[[88]](#cite_note-88) The next month the paper laid off another five people from its standards and ethics team.[[89]](#cite_note-89) In April, the paper laid off at least 11 people from its video and social media desks.[[90]](#cite_note-90) In May, the *Journal* cut six editorial staff positions from its [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong") bureau and another two reporter jobs in [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore"). Moving forward the paper will shift its focus in the region from Hong Kong to Singapore with new the creation of several new jobs at that bureau.[[91]](#cite_note-91) More staff were laid off a few weeks later amid further restructuring, including at least eight reporters.[[92]](#cite_note-92)

### Recent milestones

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wall_Street_Journal&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Recent milestones")]

* *WSJ Noted.*, a monthly digital magazine, launches on June 30, 2020, in a bid to attract younger readers.[[93]](#cite_note-noted-nieman-93)
* Reaches 3 million subscribers in May 2020[[94]](#cite_note-biz-news-3m-94)
* WSJ Live became available on mobile devices in September 2011.[[95]](#cite_note-95)
* *WSJ Weekend*, the weekend newspaper, expanded September 2010, with two new sections: "Off Duty" and "Review".[[96]](#cite_note-96)[[97]](#cite_note-97)
* "Greater New York", a stand-alone, full-color section dedicated to the [New York metro](/wiki/New_York_metropolitan_area "New York metropolitan area") area, ran from April 2010 until July 2021.[[98]](#cite_note-98)[[83]](#cite_note-nyt-greater-ny-83)
* *The Wall Street Journal*'s San Francisco Bay Area Edition, which focuses on local news and events, launched on November 5, 2009, appearing locally each Thursday in the print *Journal* and every day online at WSJ.com/SF.[[99]](#cite_note-99)
* *WSJ Weekend*, formerly called *Saturday's Weekend Edition*: September 2005.[[100]](#cite_note-100)
* Launch of *Today's Journal*, which included both the addition of *Personal Journal* and color capacity to the *Journal*: April 2002.[[101]](#cite_note-101)
* Launch of *The Wall Street Journal Sunday*: September 12, 1999. A four-page print supplement of original investing news, market reports and personal-finance advice that ran in the business sections of other U.S. newspapers. *WSJ Sunday* circulation peaked in 2005 with 84 newspapers reaching nearly 11 million homes. The publication ceased on February 7, 2015.[[102]](#cite_note-102)
* *Friday Journal*, formerly called *First Weekend Journal*: March 20, 1998.[[103]](#cite_note-wsjpro.com_2012-103)
* WSJ.com launched in April 1996.[[104]](#cite_note-104)
* First three-section *Journal*: October 1988.[[103]](#cite_note-wsjpro.com_2012-103)
* First two-section *Journal*: June 1980.[[103]](#cite_note-wsjpro.com_2012-103)

## Features and operations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wall_Street_Journal&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Features and operations")]

Since 1980, the *Journal* has been published in multiple sections. The *Journal* increased its maximum issue length to 96 pages, including up to 24 color pages, in 2002.[[105]](#cite_note-105)

As of 2012[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=The_Wall_Street_Journal&action=edit), *The Wall Street Journal* had a global news staff of around 2,000 journalists in
85 news bureaus across 51 countries.[[106]](#cite_note-djfactsheet-106)[[107]](#cite_note-107) As of 2012[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=The_Wall_Street_Journal&action=edit), it had 26 printing plants.[[106]](#cite_note-djfactsheet-106) Its Asia headquarters is in Hong Kong, but will move to Singapore after it stated it would do so in 2024.[[108]](#cite_note-108)

Regularly scheduled sections are:

* Section One: Every day; corporate news, as well as political and economic reporting and the opinion pages
* Marketplace: Monday through Friday; coverage of health, technology, [media](/wiki/Mass_media "Mass media"), and [marketing](/wiki/Marketing "Marketing") industries (the second section was launched June 23, 1980)
* Money and Investing: Every day; covers and analyzes international financial markets (the third section was launched October 3, 1988)
* Personal Journal: Published Tuesday through Thursday; covers personal investments, [careers](/wiki/Career "Career") and cultural pursuits (the section was introduced April 9, 2002)
* Off Duty: Published Saturdays in WSJ Weekend; focuses on fashion, food, design, travel and gear/tech. The section was launched September 25, 2010.
* Review: Published Saturdays in WSJ Weekend; focuses on essays, commentary, reviews and ideas. The section was launched September 25, 2010.
* Mansion: Published Fridays, focuses on high-end real estate. The section was launched October 5, 2012.
* WSJ Magazine: Launched in 2008 as a quarterly, this luxury magazine supplement distributed within the U.S., European and Asian editions of *The Wall Street Journal* grew to 12 issues per year in 2014.

In addition, several columnists contribute regular features to the *Journal* opinion page:

* Mondays: Americas by [Mary O'Grady](/wiki/Mary_O%27Grady "Mary O'Grady")
* Wednesdays: Business World by [Holman W. Jenkins Jr.](/wiki/Holman_W._Jenkins_Jr. "Holman W. Jenkins Jr.")
* Thursdays: Wonder Land by [Daniel Henninger](/wiki/Daniel_Henninger "Daniel Henninger")
* Fridays: Potomac Watch by [Kimberley Strassel](/wiki/Kimberley_Strassel "Kimberley Strassel")
* Weekend Edition: Rule of Law, The Weekend Interview (variety of authors), Declarations by [Peggy Noonan](/wiki/Peggy_Noonan "Peggy Noonan")

In addition to editorials and columns from the printed newspaper, wsj.com carries two daily web-only opinion columns:

* *Best of the Web Today* by [James Taranto](/wiki/James_Taranto "James Taranto"), the editor of the former OpinionJournal.com website (no subscription required).[[109]](#cite_note-109)
* *Political Diary* edited by [Holman W. Jenkins Jr.](/wiki/Holman_W._Jenkins_Jr. "Holman W. Jenkins Jr.") and featuring [John Fund](/wiki/John_Fund "John Fund") (separate subscription required).

In addition to these regular opinion pieces, on Fridays the *Journal* publishes a religion-themed op-ed, titled "Houses of Worship", written by a different author each week. Authors range from the [Dalai Lama](/wiki/Dalai_Lama "Dalai Lama") to cardinals.

### *Style & Substance*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wall_Street_Journal&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Style & Substance")]

***Style & Substance*** is a monthly bulletin on [English language](/wiki/English_language "English language") usage. Each issue discusses specific language issues from the perspective of the WSJ's copyeditors according to the newspaper's internal [stylebook](/wiki/Style_guide "Style guide"). The first issue of *Style & Substance* was published in 1987 under the direction of front page editor Paul R. Martin.[[110]](#cite_note-110) Front page editor Bill Power and online editor Jennifer Hicks succeeded him in 2013.[[111]](#cite_note-111) The *Journal* announces major stylistic changes through the bulletin, such as the newspaper's abandonment of [courtesy titles](/wiki/Courtesy_title "Courtesy title") in 2023.[[112]](#cite_note-112)

### *WSJ.*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wall_Street_Journal&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: WSJ.")]

Main article: [*WSJ Magazine*](/wiki/WSJ_Magazine "WSJ Magazine")

*WSJ.* is *The Wall Street Journal*'s luxury lifestyle magazine. Its coverage spans art, fashion, entertainment, design, food, architecture, travel and more. Sarah Ball is Editor in Chief and Omblyne Pelier is Publisher.[[113]](#cite_note-113)

Launched as a quarterly in 2008, the magazine grew to 12 issues a year for 2014.[[114]](#cite_note-114) The magazine is inserted into the weekend U.S. edition of *The Wall Street Journal* and is available on WSJ.com and in the newspaper's iPad app.

[Penélope Cruz](/wiki/Pen%C3%A9lope_Cruz "Penélope Cruz"), [Carmelo Anthony](/wiki/Carmelo_Anthony "Carmelo Anthony"), [Woody Allen](/wiki/Woody_Allen "Woody Allen"), [Scarlett Johansson](/wiki/Scarlett_Johansson "Scarlett Johansson"), [Emilia Clarke](/wiki/Emilia_Clarke "Emilia Clarke"), [Daft Punk](/wiki/Daft_Punk "Daft Punk"), and [Gisele Bündchen](/wiki/Gisele_B%C3%BCndchen "Gisele Bündchen") have all been featured on the cover.

In 2012, the magazine launched its Innovator Awards program. An extension of the November Innovators issue, the awards ceremony, held in New York City at [Museum of Modern Art](/wiki/Museum_of_Modern_Art "Museum of Modern Art"), honors visionaries across the fields of design, fashion, architecture, humanitarianism, art and technology.

In 2013, *Adweek* named *WSJ.* the "Hottest Lifestyle Magazine of the Year" in its annual Hot List.[[115]](#cite_note-115)

### OpinionJournal.com

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wall_Street_Journal&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: OpinionJournal.com")]

OpinionJournal.com

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| Type of site | News and opinion |
| Available in | English |
| Owner | *The Wall Street Journal* |
| Created by | *The Wall Street Journal* |
| [Revenue](/wiki/Revenue "Revenue") | N/A |
| URL | [opinionjournal.com](http://opinionjournal.com) |
| Commercial | Yes |
| Registration | N/A |
| Current status | Redirects to [wsj.com/news/opinion](https://wsj.com/news/opinion) |

**OpinionJournal.com** was a [website](/wiki/Website "Website") featuring content from the editorial pages of *The Wall Street Journal*. It existed separately from the news content at **wsj.com** until January 2008, when it was merged into the main website.[[116]](#cite_note-116)
The editorials (titled "Review & Outlook") reflected *The Journal*'s [conservative](/wiki/Conservatism_in_the_United_States "Conservatism in the United States") political [editorial line](#Editorial_page_and_political_stance), as did its regular [columnists](/wiki/Columnist "Columnist"), who included [Peggy Noonan](/wiki/Peggy_Noonan "Peggy Noonan"), [John Fund](/wiki/John_Fund "John Fund"), and [Daniel Henninger](/wiki/Daniel_Henninger "Daniel Henninger").

### *WSJ Noted.*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wall_Street_Journal&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: WSJ Noted.")]

On June 30, 2020, the *Journal* launched *WSJ Noted.*, a monthly digital "news and culture" magazine for subscribers aged 18–34 in a bid to attract a younger audience to the *Journal*. The magazine has a group of some 7,000 young adults who are invited to preview content, provide feedback, and join Q&As with Noted staff.[[93]](#cite_note-noted-nieman-93)

## Editorial board

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wall_Street_Journal&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Editorial board")]

Main article: [Editorial board at *The Wall Street Journal*](/wiki/Editorial_board_at_The_Wall_Street_Journal "Editorial board at The Wall Street Journal")

*The Wall Street Journal* editorial board members oversee the *Journal*'s editorial page, dictating the tone and direction of the newspaper's opinion section. *The Wall Street Journal* does not provide details on the exact duties of board members.

Every Saturday and Sunday, three editorial page writers and host [Paul Gigot](/wiki/Paul_Gigot "Paul Gigot"), editor of the editorial page, appear on [Fox News Channel](/wiki/Fox_News_Channel "Fox News Channel")'s *[Journal Editorial Report](/wiki/Journal_Editorial_Report "Journal Editorial Report")*, where they discuss current issues with a variety of guests. As editors of the editorial page, [Vermont C. Royster](/wiki/Vermont_C._Royster "Vermont C. Royster") (1958–1971) and [Robert Bartley](/wiki/Robert_Bartley "Robert Bartley") (1972–2000) provided a [conservative](/wiki/Conservatism_in_the_United_States "Conservatism in the United States") interpretation of the news on a daily basis.[[117]](#cite_note-vetter-117)

Contrasts have been noted between the *Journal*'s news reporting and its editorial pages.[[118]](#cite_note-Smith_10/25/2020-118) "While *Journal* reporters keep busy informing readers," wrote one reporter in 1982, "*Journal* editorial writers put forth views that often contradict the paper's best reporting and news analysis."[[119]](#cite_note-119) Two summaries published in 1995 by the progressive blog [Fairness and Accuracy in Reporting](/wiki/Fairness_and_Accuracy_in_Reporting "Fairness and Accuracy in Reporting"), and in 1996 by the *[Columbia Journalism Review](/wiki/Columbia_Journalism_Review "Columbia Journalism Review")*[[120]](#cite_note-120) criticized the *Journal*'s editorial page for inaccuracy during the 1980s and 1990s. One reference work in 2011 described the editorial pages as "rigidly neoconservative" while noting that the news coverage "has enjoyed a sterling reputation among readers of all political stripes".[[121]](#cite_note-121)

In July 2020, more than 280 *Journal* journalists and Dow Jones staff members wrote a letter to new publisher [Almar Latour](/wiki/Almar_Latour "Almar Latour") to criticize the opinion pages' "lack of [fact-checking](/wiki/Fact-checking "Fact-checking") and transparency, and its apparent disregard for evidence", adding that "opinion articles often make assertions that are contradicted by *WSJ* reporting."[[122]](#cite_note-wsj-opinion-news-122)[[123]](#cite_note-123) The editorial board responded that its opinion pages "won't wilt under cancel-culture pressure" and that the objective of the editorial content is to be independent of the *Journal*'s news content and offer alternative views to "the uniform progressive views that dominate nearly all of today's media."[[124]](#cite_note-124) The board's response did not address issues regarding fact-checking that had been raised in the letter.[[125]](#cite_note-125)

### Editorial positions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wall_Street_Journal&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Editorial positions")]

#### Economic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wall_Street_Journal&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Economic")]

In the 1900s, the *Journal* supported the [antitrust](/wiki/United_States_antitrust_law "United States antitrust law") efforts of President [Theodore Roosevelt](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt "Theodore Roosevelt"). However, after the ownership change from Dow to Barron, the *Journal* became far more unequivocally supportive of free-market economics in the 1920s.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScharff1986[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage16mode2upq22dailysermons22_16]-28) One editorial in March 1928 criticized Congressional efforts to regulate the securities industry: "People who know nothing about credit, surplus bank funds, collateral, call loans or anything else germane to the question profess to be terrified when the Stock Exchange loans attain the figure of $4 billion or more."[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScharff1986[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage28mode2upq22Peoplewhoknownothing22_28-29]-126)

On April 14, 1932, the *Journal* published a commentary by former editor [Thomas F. Woodlock](/wiki/Thomas_F._Woodlock "Thomas F. Woodlock") criticizing the [Glass–Steagall](/wiki/Glass%E2%80%93Steagall_legislation "Glass–Steagall legislation") banking regulation bill: "There are those who cannot endure the sight of autonomous securities markets beyond the control of legislatures, bureaucrats, and, in fact, of courts."[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScharff1986[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage36mode2upq22numberofpageswouldalsofall22_36]-32)

In the 1980s, the newspaper's editorial page was particularly influential as the leading voice for [supply-side economics](/wiki/Supply-side_economics "Supply-side economics"). Under the editorship of [Robert L. Bartley](/wiki/Robert_L._Bartley "Robert L. Bartley"), it expounded at length on economic concepts such as the [Laffer curve](/wiki/Laffer_curve "Laffer curve"), and how a decrease in certain marginal tax rates and the [capital gains tax](/wiki/Capital_gains_tax_in_the_United_States "Capital gains tax in the United States") could allegedly increase overall tax revenue by generating more economic activity.[[127]](#cite_note-bartley-obit-127)

In the economic argument of [exchange rate regimes](/wiki/Exchange_rate_regime "Exchange rate regime") (one of the most divisive issues among economists), the *Journal* has a tendency to support [fixed exchange rates](/wiki/Fixed_exchange_rate "Fixed exchange rate") over [floating exchange rates](/wiki/Floating_exchange_rate "Floating exchange rate").[[128]](#cite_note-128)

#### Political

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wall_Street_Journal&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Political")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Interview_Wall_Street_Journal_%286425462779%29.jpg/250px-Interview_Wall_Street_Journal_%286425462779%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Interview_Wall_Street_Journal_(6425462779).jpg)

[Mark Rutte](/wiki/Mark_Rutte "Mark Rutte") (on right), prime minister of the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands"), being interviewed by The *Journal* in 2011

The *Journal*'s editorial pages and columns, run separately from the news pages, have a [conservative](/wiki/Conservatism_in_the_United_States "Conservatism in the United States") bent and are highly influential in establishment conservative circles.[[8]](#cite_note-Vernon_2017-8) Despite this, the *Journal* refrains from endorsing candidates and has not endorsed a candidate since 1928.[[129]](#cite_note-129)

The editorial board has long argued for a pro-business [immigration policy](/wiki/Immigration_policy_of_the_United_States "Immigration policy of the United States").[[130]](#cite_note-130)

The *Journal*'s editorial page was seen as critical of many aspects of [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama")'s presidency. In particular, it has been a prominent critic of the [Affordable Care Act](/wiki/Patient_Protection_and_Affordable_Care_Act "Patient Protection and Affordable Care Act") legislation passed in 2010, and has featured many opinion columns attacking various aspects of the bill.[[131]](#cite_note-131) The *Journal*'s editorial page has also criticized the [Obama administration's energy policies](/wiki/Energy_policy_of_the_Obama_administration "Energy policy of the Obama administration") and foreign policy.[[132]](#cite_note-132)[[133]](#cite_note-133)[[134]](#cite_note-134)

On October 25, 2017, the editorial board called for Special Counsel [Robert Mueller](/wiki/Robert_Mueller "Robert Mueller") to resign from the investigation into [Russian interference in the 2016 United States elections](/wiki/Russian_interference_in_the_2016_United_States_elections "Russian interference in the 2016 United States elections") and accused [Hillary Clinton's 2016 presidential campaign](/wiki/Hillary_Clinton_2016_presidential_campaign "Hillary Clinton 2016 presidential campaign") of colluding with Russia.[[135]](#cite_note-135)[[136]](#cite_note-136) In December 2017, the editorial board repeated its calls for Mueller's resignation.[[137]](#cite_note-Business_Insider-137)[[138]](#cite_note-Frej_2017-138) The editorials by the editorial board caused fractures within *The Wall Street Journal*, as reporters say that the editorials undermine the paper's credibility.[[137]](#cite_note-Business_Insider-137)[[138]](#cite_note-Frej_2017-138)[[139]](#cite_note-139)

In October 2021, the Journal published a letter from former President Donald Trump in the Letters to the Editor section of the editorial pages. Other news sources described the contents of the letter as false and debunked claims about the [2020 presidential election](/wiki/2020_United_States_presidential_election "2020 United States presidential election").[[140]](#cite_note-140)[[141]](#cite_note-141)[[142]](#cite_note-142) The next day, the editorial board published their own critique of Trump's letter.[[143]](#cite_note-143)

#### Scientific

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wall_Street_Journal&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Scientific")]

The *Journal* editorial pages were described as a "forum for [climate change denial](/wiki/Climate_change_denial "Climate change denial")" in 2011 due to columns that attacked climate scientists and accused them of engaging in fraud.[[144]](#cite_note-144)[[145]](#cite_note-145) A 2011 study found that the *Journal* was alone among major American print news media in how, mainly in its editorial pages, it adopted a [false balance](/wiki/False_balance "False balance") that overplayed the uncertainty in climate science or denied anthropogenic climate change altogether.[[146]](#cite_note-Feldman-146) That year, the [Associated Press](/wiki/Associated_Press "Associated Press") described the *Journal*'s editorial pages as "a place friendly to climate change skeptics".[[147]](#cite_note-147) In 2013, the editorial board and other opinion writers vocally criticized President [Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama")'s plan to address climate change, mostly without mentioning climate science.[[148]](#cite_note-148) A 2015 study found *The Wall Street Journal* was the newspaper that was least likely to present negative [effects of global warming](/wiki/Effects_of_global_warming "Effects of global warming") among several newspapers. It was also the most likely to present negative economic framing when discussing [climate change mitigation](/wiki/Climate_change_mitigation "Climate change mitigation") policies, tending to take the stance that the cost of such policies generally outweighs their benefit.[[146]](#cite_note-Feldman-146)

[Climate Feedback](/wiki/Climate_Feedback "Climate Feedback"), a fact-checking website on media coverage of climate science, determined that multiple opinion articles range between "low" and "very low" in terms of scientific credibility.[[149]](#cite_note-149)[[150]](#cite_note-150) The [Partnership for Responsible Growth](/wiki/Partnership_for_Responsible_Growth "Partnership for Responsible Growth") stated in 2016 that 14% of the guest editorials on climate change presented the results of "mainstream climate science", while the majority did not. The Partnership also determined that none of the 201 editorials concerning climate change that were published in *The Wall Street Journal* since 1997 conceded that the burning of [fossil fuels](/wiki/Fossil_fuel "Fossil fuel") is the main [cause of climate change](/wiki/Cause_of_climate_change "Cause of climate change").[[151]](#cite_note-151)

In the 1980s and 1990s, the *Journal* published numerous opinion columns opposing and misrepresenting the scientific consensus on the harms of [second-hand smoke](/wiki/Passive_smoking "Passive smoking"),[[152]](#cite_note-152)[[153]](#cite_note-153)[[154]](#cite_note-154) [acid rain](/wiki/Acid_rain "Acid rain"), and [ozone depletion](/wiki/Ozone_depletion "Ozone depletion"),[[155]](#cite_note-155) in addition to public policy efforts to curb pesticides and asbestos.[[156]](#cite_note-handful-156)[[157]](#cite_note-157)[[158]](#cite_note-158)[[159]](#cite_note-159)[[160]](#cite_note-160) The *Journal* later recognized that efforts to curb acid rain through cap-and-trade had been successful, a decade after the Clean Air Act Amendments.[[161]](#cite_note-161)
