The *Journal*'s reporting has been described as "[small-c conservatism](/wiki/Small-c_conservatism "Small-c conservatism")".[[118]](#cite_note-Smith_10/25/2020-118) Some of the *Journal*'s former editors and reporters, such as [Sarah Ellison](/wiki/Sarah_Ellison "Sarah Ellison") (a *Washington Post* reporter as of October 2025),[[162]](#cite_note-162) say that the paper has adopted a more conservative tone since [Rupert Murdoch](/wiki/Rupert_Murdoch "Rupert Murdoch")'s purchase.[[163]](#cite_note-163)[[164]](#cite_note-164)[[165]](#cite_note-165)

### Bias in news pages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wall_Street_Journal&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Bias in news pages")]

#### Pre-Murdoch ownership

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wall_Street_Journal&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Pre-Murdoch ownership")]

Its editors stress the independence and impartiality of their reporters.[[74]](#cite_note-Crovitz-74) According to CNN in 2007, the *Journal*'s "newsroom staff has a reputation for non-partisan reporting."[[166]](#cite_note-166) Ben Smith of the *New York Times* described the *Journal*'s news reporting as "small-c [conservative]", and noted that its readership leans further to the right than that of other major newspapers.[[118]](#cite_note-Smith_10/25/2020-118)

Under the ownership of Clarence W. Barron, the *Journal* generally restricted editorializing to its opinion pages, but a 1922 series of news articles described the [organized labor](/wiki/Organized_labor "Organized labor") movement as having "one of the most sordid records of humanity".[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScharff1986[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage16mode2upq22dailysermons22_16-17]-167)

In a 2004 study, Tim Groseclose and [Jeffrey Milyo](/wiki/Jeffrey_Milyo "Jeffrey Milyo") argue the *Journal*'s news pages have a pro-liberal bias because they more often quote liberal think tanks. They calculated the ideological attitude of news reports in 20 media outlets by counting the frequency they cited particular [think tanks](/wiki/Think_tank "Think tank") and comparing that to the frequency that legislators cited the same think tanks. They found that the news reporting of the *Journal* was the most liberal (more liberal than [NPR](/wiki/NPR "NPR") or *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")*). The study did not factor in editorials.[[168]](#cite_note-168) [Mark Liberman](/wiki/Mark_Liberman "Mark Liberman") criticized the model used to calculate bias in the study and argued that the model unequally affected liberals and conservatives and that "the model starts with a very peculiar assumption about the relationship between political opinion and the choice of authorities to cite." The authors assume that "think tank ideology ... only matters to liberals."[[169]](#cite_note-169)

The company's planned and eventual acquisition by [News Corp](/wiki/News_Corp "News Corp") in 2007 led to significant media criticism and discussion
about whether the news pages would exhibit a rightward slant under [Rupert Murdoch](/wiki/Rupert_Murdoch "Rupert Murdoch").[[170]](#cite_note-170) An August 1, 2007, editorial responded to the questions by asserting that Murdoch intended to "maintain the values and integrity of the *Journal*".[[171]](#cite_note-171)

#### During Trump presidency

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wall_Street_Journal&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: During Trump presidency")]

In 2016 and 2017, the *Journal* leadership under Baker came under fire from critics[*[who?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*], who viewed the paper's coverage of President [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") as too timid.[[172]](#cite_note-Ember-172) Particularly controversial was the *Journal*'s November 2016 front-page headline that repeated Trump's false claim that "millions of people" had voted illegally in the [election](/wiki/United_States_elections,_2016 "United States elections, 2016"), only noting that the statement was "without corroboration".[[172]](#cite_note-Ember-172)

Also controversial was a January 2017 note from Baker to *Journal* editors, directing them to avoid using the phrase "seven majority-Muslim countries" when writing about [Trump's executive order on travel and immigration](/wiki/Executive_Order_13769 "Executive Order 13769"); Baker later sent a follow-up note "clarifying that there was 'no ban'" on the phrase, "but that the publication should 'always be careful that this term is not offered as the only description of the countries covered under the ban.'"[[172]](#cite_note-Ember-172)

At a town-hall-style meeting with *Journal* staff in February 2017, Baker defended the paper's coverage, saying that it was objective and protected the paper from being "dragged into the political process" through a dispute with the Trump administration.[[172]](#cite_note-Ember-172)

On February 19, 2020, China announced the revoking of the press credentials of three *Wall Street Journal* reporters based in Beijing. China accused the paper of failing to apologize for publishing articles that criticized China's efforts to fight the COVID-19 pandemic, and failing to investigate and punish those responsible.[[173]](#cite_note-173)

In June 2020, following the [murder of George Floyd](/wiki/Murder_of_George_Floyd "Murder of George Floyd") and [subsequent protests](/wiki/George_Floyd_protests "George Floyd protests"), journalists at the *Journal* sent a letter to editor in chief [Matt Murray](/wiki/Matt_Murray_(journalist) "Matt Murray (journalist)") demanding changes to the way the paper covers race, policing and finance. The reporters stated that they "frequently meet resistance when trying to reflect the accounts and voices of workers, residents or customers, with some editors voicing heightened skepticism of those sources' credibility compared with executives, government officials or other entities".[[174]](#cite_note-174)

## Notable stories

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wall_Street_Journal&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Notable stories")]

The *Journal* has won 39 [Pulitzer Prizes](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize "Pulitzer Prize") in its history. Staff journalists who led some of the newspaper's best-known coverage teams have later published books that summarized and extended their reporting.

### 1939: World War I battleships

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wall_Street_Journal&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: 1939: World War I battleships")]

In 1939, [Vermont C. Royster](/wiki/Vermont_C._Royster "Vermont C. Royster") wrote a series of articles showing the length of time required for [World War I battleships](/wiki/List_of_battleships_of_World_War_I "List of battleships of World War I") to be ready for duty. One story summarized the process of manufacturing armor plates. The U.S. Navy accused Royster, then a Naval Reserve officer, of using classified information for his reporting. Royster said that he obtained the information from *[Encyclopedia Britannica](/wiki/Encyclopedia_Britannica "Encyclopedia Britannica")*.[[175]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScharff1986[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage58mode2upq22worldwaribattleship22_59]-[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage60mode2upqbritannica_60]-175)

### 1952: Empire Mail Order

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wall_Street_Journal&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: 1952: Empire Mail Order")]

In October 1952, the Chicago bureau of the *Journal* received a press release announcing that [Howard Hughes](/wiki/Howard_Hughes "Howard Hughes") sold his controlling interest in [RKO Pictures](/wiki/RKO_Pictures "RKO Pictures") to the Empire Mail Order Company. Managing editor Henry Gemmill led reporting efforts that resulted in articles in late 1952 exposing links between Empire Mail Order and organized crime. Following these stories, Hughes canceled the RKO sale. These stories also led to the first major industry award for the *Journal*, a [Sigma Delta Chi](/wiki/Society_of_Professional_Journalists "Society of Professional Journalists") public service award for these stories.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScharff1986[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage120mode2upq22howardhughes22_121]-[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage122mode2upqrko_122]-44)

### 1954: Automobile models

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wall_Street_Journal&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: 1954: Automobile models")]

On May 28, 1954, the *Journal* published a front-page story by John Williams revealing designs of 1955 automobiles by [Ford](/wiki/Ford_Motor_Company "Ford Motor Company"), [Pontiac](/wiki/Pontiac_(automobile) "Pontiac (automobile)"), and other auto makers. Williams had talked to factory workers and auto executives for several weeks. The story revealed that the cars would have larger engines and wrap-around windshields. The inclusion of drawings was notable for being a rare use of graphics by the *Journal* of the time. Nearly a week later, [General Motors](/wiki/General_Motors "General Motors") canceled its nearly $250,000 in advertising with the *Journal*; GM president [Harlow Curtice](/wiki/Harlow_Curtice "Harlow Curtice") accused the *Journal* of copyright infringement and breach of confidentiality.[[176]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScharff1986[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage126mode2upq22Williamsdidnotattempt22_126]-[httpsarchiveorgdetailsworldlypowermaki0000schapage126mode2upq22may28195422_127]-176) [Richard Tofel](/wiki/Richard_Tofel "Richard Tofel") of [ProPublica](/wiki/ProPublica "ProPublica") commented on this story in 2015: "The little-remembered incident helped establish the notion that news organizations could and should preserve their independence from advertisers."[[177]](#cite_note-177)

### 1973: Spiro Agnew bribery

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wall_Street_Journal&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: 1973: Spiro Agnew bribery")]

On August 7, 1973, in an article by [Jerry Landauer](/wiki/Jerry_Landauer "Jerry Landauer"), the *Journal* was the first publication to report that U.S. Vice President [Spiro Agnew](/wiki/Spiro_Agnew "Spiro Agnew") was under federal investigation on bribery, extortion, and tax fraud charges. Agnew released a statement confirming the investigation on the night before the *Journal* article was published.[[178]](#cite_note-178)[[179]](#cite_note-179) After pleading no contest to one charge of income tax evasion, Agnew was sentenced to a $10,000 fine and three-year suspended jail sentence. On the same day of his sentencing, Agnew resigned as vice president on October 10, 1973.[[180]](#cite_note-180)

### 1984: CIA mining in Nicaragua

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wall_Street_Journal&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: 1984: CIA mining in Nicaragua")]

See also: [CIA activities in Nicaragua](/wiki/CIA_activities_in_Nicaragua "CIA activities in Nicaragua")

On April 6, 1984, the *Journal* first revealed, in a story by David Rogers, that the [Central Intelligence Agency](/wiki/Central_Intelligence_Agency "Central Intelligence Agency") had placed [acoustic mines](/wiki/Acoustic_mine "Acoustic mine") on harbors in Nicaragua, following initial belief that opponents of the [Sandinista](/wiki/Sandinista "Sandinista") government had been responsible.[[181]](#cite_note-Cannon-181)[[182]](#cite_note-182) As [Lou Cannon](/wiki/Lou_Cannon "Lou Cannon") recounted in his 1991 book *President Reagan: The Role of a Lifetime*, this story led to "an international outcry".[[181]](#cite_note-Cannon-181) Nearly week after this *Journal* story, in a speech on the Senate floor and a stern letter to CIA director [William J. Casey](/wiki/William_J._Casey "William J. Casey"), U.S. Senator [Barry Goldwater](/wiki/Barry_Goldwater "Barry Goldwater") (Republican of Arizona) condemned the lack of disclosure by the Reagan administration about the mining actions.[[183]](#cite_note-183)[[184]](#cite_note-184) By an 84–12 margin, the Senate passed a "sense of the Congress" resolution introduced by Senator [Ted Kennedy](/wiki/Ted_Kennedy "Ted Kennedy") (Democrat of Massachusetts) "against the mining of Nicaraguan ports and the withdrawal of World Court jurisdiction over Central America."[[185]](#cite_note-185)

### 1987: RJR Nabisco buyout

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wall_Street_Journal&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: 1987: RJR Nabisco buyout")]

In 1987, a bidding war ensued between several financial firms for tobacco and food giant [RJR Nabisco](/wiki/RJR_Nabisco "RJR Nabisco"). Bryan Burrough and John Helyar documented the events in more than two dozen *Journal* articles. Burrough and Helyar later used these articles as the basis of a bestselling book, *[Barbarians at the Gate: The Fall of RJR Nabisco](/wiki/Barbarians_at_the_Gate:_The_Fall_of_RJR_Nabisco "Barbarians at the Gate: The Fall of RJR Nabisco")*, which was turned into a [film](/wiki/Barbarians_at_the_Gate_(film) "Barbarians at the Gate (film)") for HBO.

### 1988: Insider trading

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wall_Street_Journal&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: 1988: Insider trading")]

In the 1980s, then-*Journal* reporter [James B. Stewart](/wiki/James_B._Stewart "James B. Stewart") brought national attention to the illegal practice of [insider trading](/wiki/Insider_trading "Insider trading"). He was awarded the [Pulitzer Prize](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize "Pulitzer Prize") in explanatory journalism in 1988, which he shared with [Daniel Hertzberg](/wiki/Daniel_Hertzberg "Daniel Hertzberg"),[[186]](#cite_note-186) who went on to serve as the paper's senior deputy managing editor before resigning in 2009. Stewart expanded on this theme in his 1991 book, *[Den of Thieves](/wiki/Den_of_Thieves_(Stewart_book) "Den of Thieves (Stewart book)")*.[[187]](#cite_note-187)

### 1997: AIDS treatment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wall_Street_Journal&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: 1997: AIDS treatment")]

David Sanford, a Page One features editor who was infected with [HIV](/wiki/HIV "HIV") in 1982 in a bathhouse, wrote a front-page personal account of how, with the assistance of improved treatments for HIV, he went from planning his death to planning his retirement.[[188]](#cite_note-188) He and six other reporters wrote about the new treatments, political and economic issues, and won the 1997 [Pulitzer Prize for National Reporting](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize_for_National_Reporting "Pulitzer Prize for National Reporting") about [AIDS](/wiki/HIV/AIDS "HIV/AIDS").[[189]](#cite_note-189)

### 2000: Enron

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wall_Street_Journal&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: 2000: Enron")]

[Jonathan Weil](/wiki/Jonathan_Weil "Jonathan Weil"), a reporter at the Dallas bureau of *The Wall Street Journal*, is credited with first breaking the story of financial abuses at [Enron](/wiki/Enron "Enron") in September 2000.[[190]](#cite_note-190) [Rebecca Smith](/wiki/Rebecca_Smith_(journalist) "Rebecca Smith (journalist)") and [John R. Emshwiller](/wiki/John_R._Emshwiller "John R. Emshwiller") reported on the story regularly,[[191]](#cite_note-191) and wrote a book, *24 Days*. In October 2021, the *Journal* released Bad Bets, a podcast that recounted the papers reporting on Enron.[[192]](#cite_note-192)

### 2001: 9/11

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wall_Street_Journal&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: 2001: 9/11")]

The *Journal* claims to have sent the first news report, on the Dow Jones wire, of a plane crashing into the [World Trade Center](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_(1973%E2%80%932001) "World Trade Center (1973–2001)") on [September 11, 2001](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks").[[193]](#cite_note-193) Its headquarters, at [One World Financial Center](/wiki/One_World_Financial_Center "One World Financial Center"), was severely damaged by the collapse of the World Trade Center just across the street.[[194]](#cite_note-The_Eye_of_the_Storm-194) Top editors worried that they might miss publishing the first issue for the first time in the paper's 112-year history. They relocated to a makeshift office at an editor's home, while sending most of the staff to Dow Jones's [South Brunswick Township, New Jersey](/wiki/South_Brunswick_Township,_New_Jersey "South Brunswick Township, New Jersey"), corporate campus.[[195]](#cite_note-Harris_2011-195) The paper was on the stands the next day, albeit in scaled-down form. The front page included a first-hand account of the Twin Towers' collapse written by then-Foreign Editor John Bussey.[[194]](#cite_note-The_Eye_of_the_Storm-194) Holed up in a ninth-floor office next to the towers, he phoned in live reports to [CNBC](/wiki/CNBC "CNBC") as the towers burned.[[194]](#cite_note-The_Eye_of_the_Storm-194) He narrowly escaped serious injury when the first tower collapsed, shattering all the windows in the *Journal*'s offices and filling them with dust and debris. The *Journal* won a [2002 Pulitzer Prize](/wiki/2002_Pulitzer_Prize "2002 Pulitzer Prize") in [Breaking News Reporting](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize_for_Breaking_News_Reporting "Pulitzer Prize for Breaking News Reporting") for that day's stories.[[196]](#cite_note-196)[[195]](#cite_note-Harris_2011-195)

The *Journal* subsequently conducted a worldwide investigation of the causes and significance of 9/11, using contacts it had developed while covering business in the Arab world. In [Kabul, Afghanistan](/wiki/Kabul,_Afghanistan "Kabul, Afghanistan"), reporters Alan Cullison and Andrew Higgins bought a pair of looted computers that [Al Qaeda](/wiki/Al_Qaeda "Al Qaeda") leaders had used to plan assassinations, chemical and biological attacks, and mundane daily activities. The encrypted files were decrypted and translated.[[197]](#cite_note-197)[[198]](#cite_note-198) It was during this coverage that terrorists kidnapped and killed *Journal* reporter [Daniel Pearl](/wiki/Daniel_Pearl "Daniel Pearl").

### 2007: Stock option scandal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wall_Street_Journal&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: 2007: Stock option scandal")]

In 2007, the paper won the [Pulitzer Prize for Public Service](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize_for_Public_Service "Pulitzer Prize for Public Service"), for exposing companies that illegally [backdate stock options](/wiki/Options_backdating "Options backdating") they awarded executives to increase their value.

### 2015–present: Theranos investigation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wall_Street_Journal&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: 2015–present: Theranos investigation")]

In 2015, a report written by the *Journal*'s [John Carreyrou](/wiki/John_Carreyrou "John Carreyrou") alleged that blood testing company [Theranos](/wiki/Theranos "Theranos")' technology was faulty and founder [Elizabeth Holmes](/wiki/Elizabeth_Holmes "Elizabeth Holmes") was misleading investors.[[199]](#cite_note-199)[[200]](#cite_note-Bilton-200)[[201]](#cite_note-201) According to *[Vanity Fair](/wiki/Vanity_Fair_(magazine) "Vanity Fair (magazine)")*, "a damning report published in *The Wall Street Journal* had alleged that the company was, in effect, a sham—that its vaunted core technology was actually faulty and that Theranos administered almost all of its blood tests using competitors' equipment."[[200]](#cite_note-Bilton-200) The *Journal* has subsequently published several more reports questioning Theranos' and Holmes' credibility.[[202]](#cite_note-202)[[203]](#cite_note-203) In May 2018, Carreyrou released a book about Theranos, *[Bad Blood](/wiki/Bad_Blood:_Secrets_and_Lies_in_a_Silicon_Valley_Startup "Bad Blood: Secrets and Lies in a Silicon Valley Startup")*.[[204]](#cite_note-204)

[Rupert Murdoch](/wiki/Rupert_Murdoch "Rupert Murdoch")—at the time a major investor in Theranos and owner of the *Journal*—lost approximately $100 million in his investments in Theranos.[[205]](#cite_note-205)

### 2018–present: Investigation into Stormy Daniels payment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wall_Street_Journal&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: 2018–present: Investigation into Stormy Daniels payment")]

Main article: [Stormy Daniels–Donald Trump scandal](/wiki/Stormy_Daniels%E2%80%93Donald_Trump_scandal "Stormy Daniels–Donald Trump scandal")

On January 12, 2018, [Michael Rothfeld](/wiki/Michael_Rothfeld "Michael Rothfeld") and [Joe Palazzolo](/wiki/Joe_Palazzolo "Joe Palazzolo") reported in *The Wall Street Journal* that during the [2016 presidential campaign](/wiki/2016_United_States_presidential_election "2016 United States presidential election"), then-candidate [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump")'s personal lawyer, [Michael Cohen](/wiki/Michael_Cohen_(lawyer) "Michael Cohen (lawyer)") coordinated a $130,000 payment to [Stormy Daniels](/wiki/Stormy_Daniels "Stormy Daniels") for her silence regarding an alleged affair. In subsequent reports, the method of payment and many other details were extensively covered. In April of that year, [FBI](/wiki/Federal_Bureau_of_Investigation "Federal Bureau of Investigation") agents stormed Cohen's home, seizing records related to the transaction.[[206]](#cite_note-206) On August 21, 2018, Cohen pleaded guilty to eight counts including campaign finance violations in connection with the Daniels payment.[[207]](#cite_note-207) The coverage earned the *Journal* the 2019 [Pulitzer Prize for National Reporting](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize_for_National_Reporting "Pulitzer Prize for National Reporting").[[208]](#cite_note-208)

### 2021: Facebook company files leak

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wall_Street_Journal&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: 2021: Facebook company files leak")]

This section is an excerpt from [2021 Facebook leak](/wiki/2021_Facebook_leak "2021 Facebook leak").[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=2021_Facebook_leak&action=edit)]

In 2021, an internal [document leak](/wiki/Document_leak "Document leak") from the company then known as Facebook (now [Meta Platforms](/wiki/Meta_Platforms "Meta Platforms"), or Meta) showed it was aware of harmful societal effects from its platforms, yet persisted in prioritizing profit over addressing these harms. The leak, released by [whistleblower](/wiki/Whistleblower "Whistleblower") [Frances Haugen](/wiki/Frances_Haugen "Frances Haugen"), resulted in reporting from *The Wall Street Journal* in September, as [*The Facebook Files*](/wiki/2021_Facebook_leak "2021 Facebook leak") series, as well as the *Facebook Papers*, by a consortium of news outlets the next months.

Primarily, the reports revealed that, based on internally-commissioned studies, the company was fully aware of negative impacts on teenage users of [Instagram](/wiki/Instagram "Instagram"), and the contribution of [Facebook](/wiki/Facebook "Facebook") activity to violence in developing countries. Other takeaways of the leak include the impact of the company's platforms on spreading false information, and Facebook's policy of promoting inflammatory posts.
Furthermore, Facebook was fully aware that harmful content was being pushed through Facebook algorithms reaching young users. The types of content included posts promoting [anorexia nervosa](/wiki/Anorexia_nervosa "Anorexia nervosa") and [self-harm](/wiki/Self-harm "Self-harm") photos.

In October 2021, [Whistleblower Aid](/wiki/Whistleblower_Aid "Whistleblower Aid") filed eight anonymous [whistleblower](/wiki/Whistleblower "Whistleblower") complaints with the [U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission](/wiki/U.S._Securities_and_Exchange_Commission "U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission") (SEC) on behalf of Haugen alleging [securities fraud](/wiki/Securities_fraud "Securities fraud") by the company, after Haugen leaked the company documents the previous month.[[209]](#cite_note-2021_Facebook_leak_Zubrow_Gavrilovic_Ortiz_10-4-2021-209)[[210]](#cite_note-210)[[211]](#cite_note-211) After publicly revealing her identity on *[60 Minutes](/wiki/60_Minutes "60 Minutes")*,[[212]](#cite_note-2021_Facebook_leak_Pelley_10-4-2021-212)[[213]](#cite_note-213) Haugen testified before the [U.S. Senate Commerce Subcommittee on Consumer Protection, Product Safety, and Data Security](/wiki/United_States_Senate_Commerce_Subcommittee_on_Consumer_Protection,_Product_Safety,_and_Data_Security "United States Senate Commerce Subcommittee on Consumer Protection, Product Safety, and Data Security") about the content of the leaked documents and the complaints.[[214]](#cite_note-214) After the company renamed itself as Meta Platforms,[[215]](#cite_note-215) Whistleblower Aid filed two additional securities fraud complaints with the SEC against the company on behalf of Haugen in February 2022.[[216]](#cite_note-216)

In response to the media fallout, Facebook executives went on press tours to express Facebook's position amidst the frenzy.[[217]](#cite_note-217) Facebook also did internal damage control with employees through in person sessions and memos.[[218]](#cite_note-218) They went on to do a rebranding and changed their logo as well as their name to Meta.[[219]](#cite_note-219)

## Controversies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wall_Street_Journal&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Controversies")]

### Hong Kong Journalists Association

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wall_Street_Journal&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Hong Kong Journalists Association")]

In June 2024 *Wall Street Journal* editors learned that *WSJ* journalist [Selina Cheng](/wiki/Selina_Cheng "Selina Cheng") was a candidate for the leadership of the [Hong Kong Journalists Association](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Journalists_Association "Hong Kong Journalists Association"), a local press union. Cheng's editor demanded that she withdraw from the election and from participation in the union. However, the right to stand for election and participate in a union without employer consent are established under Hong Kong employment law. Cheng did not withdraw and was elected to the leadership role.[[220]](#cite_note-220)[[221]](#cite_note-221)[[222]](#cite_note-222)[[223]](#cite_note-223) In July 2024, the paper's chief editor Gordon Fairclough travelled to Hong Kong and fired her from her role at the *WSJ*. In response to press queries, the *WSJ* declined to comment on Cheng's case except to acknowledge there had been "restructuring". Cheng responded that the restructuring was a layoff of one person.[[224]](#cite_note-Cheng2024-224)

The paper's action against Cheng attracted criticism from media organisations, press unions and human rights proponents across the globe.[[225]](#cite_note-225)[[226]](#cite_note-Walden_2024-226)[[227]](#cite_note-227) Chinese state media, meanwhile, celebrated her sacking, with the *[Global Times](/wiki/Global_Times "Global Times")*, a tabloid owned by the [Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Party "Chinese Communist Party"), calling the press union "a malignant tumour that harms the city's safety and security".[[228]](#cite_note-228)[[226]](#cite_note-Walden_2024-226) Asked by the *[South China Morning Post](/wiki/South_China_Morning_Post "South China Morning Post")* about his views about the reason for the dismissal, Hong Kong's security minister [Chris Tang](/wiki/Chris_Tang "Chris Tang") mentioned what he saw as the possibility that the journal might have wanted not to be associated with the press union, the latter being in his view "not a well-recognized association".[[229]](#cite_note-229) Cheng said that the *Wall Street Journal* had fired her to avoid being seen as advocating press freedom in the city.[[224]](#cite_note-Cheng2024-224) In July 2024, the [NewsGuild-CWA](/wiki/NewsGuild-CWA "NewsGuild-CWA") and Independent Association of Publishers' Employees, the union for [Dow Jones](/wiki/Dow_Jones_%26_Company "Dow Jones & Company"), expressed their support for Cheng.[[230]](#cite_note-230) The latter launched a petition calling on *WSJ* to reinstate Cheng, which has garnered 335 employee signatures in support.[[231]](#cite_note-231) In November 2024, Cheng filed a civil lawsuit in Hong Kong against the newspaper over her firing.[[232]](#cite_note-232)[[233]](#cite_note-233)

In February 2025, criminal proceedings against the *WSJ* began in Hong Kong.[[234]](#cite_note-234)

[Centrist](/wiki/Centrist_camp "Centrist camp") lawmaker [Tik Chi-yuen](/wiki/Tik_Chi-yuen "Tik Chi-yuen") criticized the *Wall Street Journal* for [self-censorship](/wiki/Self-censorship "Self-censorship") and fear of offending the authorities, which seriously impacts press freedom in Hong Kong.[[235]](#cite_note-235) Former district councillor [Ben Lam](/wiki/Ben_Lam_(politician) "Ben Lam (politician)") described *WSJ*'s actions as supporting authoritarianism and compared their chief editor to [Walter Duranty](/wiki/Walter_Duranty "Walter Duranty").[[236]](#cite_note-236)

In a series of articles about News Corp, Australian online news outlet [Crikey](/wiki/Crikey "Crikey") described Cheng's firing as part of "turmoil" at the *WSJ* and that "the global purging of newsrooms is as much about creating a politically correct workforce of reliable journalistic cadres as it is about simply saving costs."[[237]](#cite_note-237) The [U.S.-China Economic and Security Review Commission](/wiki/United_States-China_Economic_and_Security_Review_Commission "United States-China Economic and Security Review Commission") annual report noted the significance of the event as demonstrating "the pressures on foreign media to self-censor in line with the [CCP](/wiki/CCP "CCP")'s requirements". It concluded that the *WSJ*'s action "calls into question claims that foreign businesses have been unaffected by the new atmosphere following the passage of the [NSL](/wiki/2020_Hong_Kong_national_security_law "2020 Hong Kong national security law") and [Article 23 Ordinance](/wiki/Safeguarding_National_Security_Ordinance "Safeguarding National Security Ordinance")".[[238]](#cite_note-238)

### President Trump feud

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wall_Street_Journal&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: President Trump feud")]

See also: [Relationship of Donald Trump and Jeffrey Epstein](/wiki/Relationship_of_Donald_Trump_and_Jeffrey_Epstein "Relationship of Donald Trump and Jeffrey Epstein") and [Donald Trump's conflict with the media § Retaliatory lawsuits and federal government actions](/wiki/Donald_Trump%27s_conflict_with_the_media#Retaliatory_lawsuits_and_federal_government_actions "Donald Trump's conflict with the media")

On July 17, 2025, the newspaper reported that, in 2003, Donald Trump had sent convicted child sex offender [Jeffrey Epstein](/wiki/Jeffrey_Epstein "Jeffrey Epstein") a 50th birthday card.[[239]](#cite_note-239) The next day, Trump (then a sitting president) sued the newspaper, alleging two counts of defamation and seeking at least $10 billion for each count. It was believed to be the first time a sitting president had ever sued a journalist or media outlet for personal defamation.[[240]](#cite_note-240) The case was assigned to Judge [Darrin Gayles](/wiki/Darrin_P._Gayles "Darrin P. Gayles").[[241]](#cite_note-241) On July 21, the *Journal* was also removed from the White House press pool for Trump's trip to Scotland.[[242]](#cite_note-242) Two days later, the *Journal* reported that Trump was informed by the Justice Department in May that his name was found in the [Epstein Files](/wiki/Epstein_Files "Epstein Files").[[243]](#cite_note-243)

### Misinformation on the assassination of Charlie Kirk

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Wall_Street_Journal&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Misinformation on the assassination of Charlie Kirk")]

This section is an excerpt from [Assassination of Charlie Kirk § "Transgender ideology" misinformation](/wiki/Assassination_of_Charlie_Kirk#"Transgender_ideology"_misinformation "Assassination of Charlie Kirk").[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Assassination_of_Charlie_Kirk&action=edit)]

Early reporting, notably in *The Wall Street Journal*, falsely said that the inscriptions on the bullets were found as having messaging related to "[transgender ideology](/wiki/Transgender_ideology "Transgender ideology")", citing what they claimed was an internal bulletin of the ATF.[[244]](#cite_note-Assassination_of_Charlie_Kirk_Migdon_2025-244) These reports were met with calls for caution from trans journalists, who said that "transgender ideology" was a term commonly used in right-wing circles to frame transgender identity as a political choice, and *The New York Times* reported that a senior law enforcement official with knowledge of the case said that the alleged bulletin had not been verified, and that it did not match other summaries of evidence; however, prominent conservative figures still seized on *The Wall Street Journal*'s report to call for [further action against the trans community](/wiki/Persecution_of_transgender_people_under_the_second_Trump_administration "Persecution of transgender people under the second Trump administration"), including banning pride flags and incarcerating all transgender people en masse.[[244]](#cite_note-Assassination_of_Charlie_Kirk_Migdon_2025-244)[[245]](#cite_note-245)

When the details of the actual messages, which did not contain any such references, were made public, the [Human Rights Campaign](/wiki/Human_Rights_Campaign "Human Rights Campaign") published an open letter demanding a retraction and a public apology for the publishing of the misinformation, saying: "This reporting was reckless and irresponsible, and it led to a wave of threats against the trans community from right-wing influencers—and a resulting wave of terror for a community that is already living in fear."[[244]](#cite_note-Assassination_of_Charlie_Kirk_Migdon_2025-244)[[246]](#cite_note-246) *The Wall Street Journal* later amended the story with a note from the editor but did not issue a retraction.[[247]](#cite_note-Assassination_of_Charlie_Kirk_Wiggins_2025-247)[[248]](#cite_note-248)[[249]](#cite_note-249)

More transgender conspiracy theories were spread after it was reported that the suspected shooter had a transgender partner, with some speculating that Robinson may have been motivated to kill Kirk because of [Kirk's views and rhetoric on transgender people](/wiki/Charlie_Kirk_and_LGBTQ_issues "Charlie Kirk and LGBTQ issues"). Jacey Thornton, an executive director of the LGBTQ advocacy group Rainbow Utah, noted, "It sounds like [they're] really stretching to find a way to tie this in to the trans community", adding that this is "very harmful to this ongoing dialogue that's happening, especially on social media".[[250]](#cite_note-Assassination_of_Charlie_Kirk_Healy_2025b-250)[[251]](#cite_note-Assassination_of_Charlie_Kirk_Caputo_2025a-251) This theorizing was further amplified by the timing of the Charlie Kirk assassination, which occurred just two weeks after a transgender individual [shot and killed](/wiki/Annunciation_Catholic_Church_shooting "Annunciation Catholic Church shooting") 2 children and wounded 21 others at [Annunciation Catholic Church](/wiki/Church_of_the_Annunciation_(Minneapolis) "Church of the Annunciation (Minneapolis)") in [Minneapolis](/wiki/Minneapolis "Minneapolis") on August 27, 2025.[[252]](#cite_note-252)
